_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Richmond County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for a county formed in 1779.
display_name: Richmond County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Served through Civil War and Reconstruction. Replaced in 1921.

record_losses.description: A fire in 1874 damaged some Richmond County records. The bound deed books and will books survived, but some loose papers — estate inventories, guardianship files, and scattered court documents — were lost. The fire was not a total destruction; the most critical genealogical records (deeds and wills) survived.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Richmond County's deed and will books survive from 1779, but some loose estate papers were lost in the 1874 fire. If {ancestor_name}'s estate inventory is missing from this period, the fire is the likely explanation.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.repository: Richmond County Register of Deeds, Rockingham; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good for bound volumes. Some loose estate papers lost in 1874 fire.
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers 1779–1874 may be incomplete.
wills_estates.repository: Richmond County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.gaps: Early marriage bonds may be incomplete.
marriages.repository: Richmond County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good. Most volumes survived the 1874 fire.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps possible in earliest years.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Richmond County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Richmond County was formed from Anson in 1779. Records before that date are in Anson County, which itself was formed in 1750 from Bladen.
substitute_sources.repository: Anson County Register of Deeds, Wadesboro; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Richmond County families with pre-Revolutionary roots. Anson County's 1750 formation gives access to mid-colonial records.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records.

substitute_sources.description: Richmond County was part of the Scottish Highland settlement zone. Emigration records, kirk session records, and published genealogies for Cape Fear Scots families are critical substitute sources.
substitute_sources.repository: National Records of Scotland, Edinburgh; UNC Chapel Hill NC Collection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Scottish surnames (McNeill, McLeod, McLean, McRae, Cameron) can be traced to specific Highland parishes using Scottish records.
substitute_sources.source: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. UNC Press, 1961.

substitute_sources.description: The Hamlet railroad junction generated employment records and depot records that document railroad workers and their families.
substitute_sources.repository: Scattered — CSX Transportation archives; NC State Archives; local historical collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Railroad employees — section hands, depot agents, shop workers — appear in railroad payroll and personnel records that supplement county courthouse records.
substitute_sources.source: Seaboard Air Line Railroad historical records.

narrative_hooks: Richmond County's records reach back to 1779 — Revolutionary War era — and the deed books and will books survived the 1874 courthouse fire that destroyed some loose papers.
Scotland County was carved from Richmond in 1899 — if {ancestor_name}'s family was in what is now Scotland County before 1899, the records are here in Rockingham.
Before Richmond County existed, this territory was Anson County — if {ancestor_name} was here in the colonial period, the trail leads to Wadesboro and the Anson County records that begin in 1750.
The Hamlet railroad junction employed hundreds — if {ancestor_name} worked for the Seaboard Air Line after 1870, railroad payroll records may document {pronoun_object} when county records cannot.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1961.
Leary, Helen F. M., ed. North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History. 2nd ed. Raleigh: North Carolina Genealogical Society, 1996.
